002-agony.finding: Registry 2 (agony) contains two analytically distinct sub-clusters: (1) the agōn- word family (6 terms: G0075, G0073, G0464, G1864, G2610, G4865), all Volitional/Will, naming directed spiritual striving rather than suffering — these are misaligned with C05's dominant suffering theme; (2) the basanizō/basanismos/basanos torment family (5 terms), all Affective/Emotional, naming eschatological and post-mortem suffering. Session B should treat these sub-clusters separately.

Groups 4652-001 and 4654-001 (H2258A cha.vol and H2258B cha.vo.lah, pledge): assigned Moral/Conscience but the inner-being filter is borderline — pledge-handling is primarily an external economic act whose moral significance is diagnostic of inner character rather than a direct inner-being engagement. Session B should examine whether the verse evidence sustains the Moral/Conscience assignment or whether these groups are better treated as externalised moral diagnostics.

Group 4651-001 (H2259 cho.vel, pilot): assigned Spiritual/God-ward but the boundary with Volitional/Will is uncertain. The helmsman figure calling the crew to prayer in extremity may name an authority/directional dimension rather than a personal God-ward orientation. Session B should examine the verse evidence to determine whether the inner-being engagement is volitional direction, spiritual appeal, or some combination.

Group 4595-001 (H4865 mish.be.tsot, filigree): assigned Character/Disposition on the basis of the metaphor of inner adornment with honour. Single-verse term with 8 set-aside verses; the description is working from a narrow evidential base. Session B should verify whether the verse evidence sustains the character/adornment reading or whether the inner-being application is marginal.

004-anger.finding: Anger governance is the paradigmatic inner-being governance challenge in Reg 4. Groups 79-004 (mastery of anger exceeds military conquest), 1554-001 (trembling restraint before God without sin), 38-001 (morally accountable anger under divine command), and 139-003 (wisdom counsel not to let anger kindle) show anger mastery as a distinct and critical sub-theme. Session B should map the vocabulary of anger-governance as a structured inner-being claim about will, character, and spiritual posture.

Two groups (1552-001: fierce anger co-existing with grief/loyalty; 37-003: righteous anger co-existing with sorrow at hardness of heart) show the inner being holding anger and love, or anger and grief, simultaneously as genuine compound affective states. Session B should examine whether Scripture treats compound anger-with-grief as a distinct inner-being mode and what theological conditions produce it.

005-anguish.finding: Group 4667-002 (H7114A qa.tser, be short — divine yielding): assigned Theological/Divine-Human. This group names divine pathos — God himself moved by the misery of his people. It is one of the clearest instances in the anguish registry of Scripture attributing an inner affective response to God. Session B should note this as a significant theological dimension within Registry 5 and consider its relationship to the broader divine-pathos theme across C05.

006-anointing.finding: Registry 6 (anointing) spans 7 dimensions across 38 groups — the widest dimensional range in C16. Session B should examine whether this breadth coheres around a unifying theme (Spirit-mediated transformation and divine endowment) or represents genuinely distinct inner-being phenomena requiring separate analytical threads.

Multiple Transformation groups in Reg 6 have GOD as dominant subject (167-001, 179-002, 4697-001, 4701-001, 4701-002). Session B should explore the full theology of God-initiated inner transformation in the anointing vocabulary — spanning Spirit-impartation, forgiveness, restoration, eschatological renewal, and judicial dulling/hardening as distinct expressions of the same divine transforming agency.

007-anxiety.finding: merimnaō (G3309) operates in two distinct inner-being registers: anxious affective worry (2709-001, 2709-002) and volitional-attentional directed care (2709-003, 350-001). Session B should map how the same word spans these two registers and what this reveals about anxiety as both an affective state and a directional inner-being act.

008-appetite.finding: Registry 8 (appetite) contributes zero desiderative groups — all 12 groups belong to the life/vitality semantic domain (Hebrew chai/chayah family). The appetite register, as extracted, has no desiderative content. Session B should establish what the appetite registry actually contributes to C04 inner-being analysis.

011-awe.finding: Awe spans both Affective/Emotional and Spiritual/God-ward dimensions. God-ward groups (1682-001, 1682-003, 703-002, 703-003) show that the deepest fear/awe content is Spiritual/God-ward rather than merely affective. Session B should examine whether awe functions as a distinct inner-being category integrating affective intensity and God-ward orientation — the two are inseparable in the data.

013-bitterness.finding: Bitterness in Reg 13 operates in two structural registers: (a) affective quality — bitter grief, anguish, affliction (something that happens to the inner person), and (b) dispositional-character state — root of bitterness, bitter jealousy as heart-state, bitter rebellion (something the inner person becomes). Session B should examine whether biblical bitterness is primarily an experience or a condition, and whether the two registers are sequential or independent.

015-boastfulness.finding: The ka.vod (glory) vocabulary in Reg 15 (boastfulness) contains its own internal corrective: divine glory (1861-001) as the proper object of inner awe, and the human act of directing glory toward God (1861-002) as the antidote to boastfulness that claims glory for itself. Session B should examine how the glory vocabulary frames the proper and improper objects of inner honour — why the same word (kavod) names both human boastfulness and divine majesty, and what this reveals about the inner-being structure of worship.

016-boldness.finding: parrēsia (G3954/3955) shows a tripartite inner-being structure: (1) access to God in prayer/worship (Spiritual/God-ward, 713-001), (2) apostolic proclamation against opposition (Character/Disposition + Spiritual/God-ward, 713-002, 714-001), (3) relational openness (Relational/Social, 713-003). Boldness is contextually differentiated — its nature shifts with its object and context. Session B should map how boldness-as-God-ward-access, boldness-as-prophetic-character, and boldness-as-relational-quality relate in the inner being.

017-bondage.finding: The bondage registry shows a consistent inner-being polarity: enslavement to disordered desires/sin/fear on one side, willing surrender to God/Christ on the other. Session B should examine this polarity as the governing analytical structure — bondage as the inner-being term for what masters the person.

018-brokenness.finding: Registry 18 (brokenness) is the most dimensionally uniform registry in C05: all 6 groups converge on Affective/Emotional. The she.ver and related vocabulary names breaking, wounding, and shattering as inner-being conditions. This sub-theme — the inner person as something that can be broken and must be healed — is a coherent and distinctive contribution within the C05 cluster that Session B should treat as a unit.

019-calling.finding: The calling registry (Reg 19) has 13 of 24 groups (54%) assigned Divine-Human Correspondence — the highest concentration of any single registry in the programme to date. The calling vocabulary is structurally a divine-human act: God calls, names, and constitutes; the human receives, responds, and is formed. Session B should examine how the human inner person is changed by the divine call, and what distinguishes the groups where HUMAN volition or emotion is the primary inner-being phenomenon from those where the divine calling act is the governing feature.

Group 727-001 (krazō — 'Abba! Father!', Rom 8:15; Gal 4:6) is the programme's clearest example of the Spirit acting from within the human inner being: the Spirit's cry is simultaneously divine and human. This is the most acute instance of Divine-Human Correspondence encountered so far — not the Spirit acting on the human from outside, but the Spirit expressing itself through and as the human inner person's deepest cry. Session B should examine whether this represents a distinct sub-type of Divine-Human Correspondence (indwelling rather than external correspondence) that may warrant distinction in the vocabulary.

020-character.finding: The dokimos root family (dokimazō / dokimos / dokimion / dokimē) encodes a coherent inner-being formation arc across four groups: dokimion (testing instrument = Transformation) → dokimazō in testing sense (character disclosed = Moral Character) → dokimē (proved character = Moral Character) → dokimos (the proved person = Moral Character); with dokimazō in its discernment sense (= Cognition) showing the same root serving both formation and ongoing moral discernment. Session B should examine whether this root family constitutes a unique formation arc in NT vocabulary — from testing instrument, through proved character, to the characterised person — and how it maps onto the programme's understanding of moral formation under trial.

023-compassion.finding: Group 733-001 (ni.chum — compassionate comfort, Reg 23) is anchored by Hos 11:8: "my heart recoils within me; my compassion grows warm and tender." This verse names the divine inner life with visceral specificity — the heart recoiling, compassion warming. Session B for Reg 23 should examine whether this visceral inner-movement language for God represents a unique category within the compassion vocabulary: divine inner-being experience described through embodied sensation. The question for Session B is whether the compassion vocabulary of Reg 23 contains a sub-pattern of "visceral inner movement" terms (splanchnizō, ni.chum, ra.cha.mim) that share a somatic/embodied description of the inner compassion movement, and whether this sub-pattern spans the divine-human boundary (both God and humans described as "moved within" by compassion).

024-condemnation.finding: H4941H mish.pat appears in both Reg 24 (condemnation) and Reg 98 (justice) — three groups each. Session B must read both registries' mish.pat groups together to form a complete inner-being profile of this term.

026-conscience.finding: The conscience (suneidesis/sunoida) groups in Reg 26 show a consistent pattern: moral self-awareness operating under divine authority — conscience as inner witness that remains subordinate to God's judgment. Session B should examine the relationship between conscience-as-moral-witness and divine judgment as the higher court.

029-contentment.finding: Both contentment terms name a stable inner condition independent of circumstances — a learned/acquired disposition of sufficiency, not a transient emotional state. Session B should examine whether contentment constitutes a distinct sub-dimension of Character/Disposition, and how it relates to the Philippians 4 context where it appears alongside joy and peace.

031-corruption.finding: Registry 31 contains two distinct sub-domains: (1) moral corruption as inner condition (4899-001, 4900-001, 4901-001, 745-001) and (2) eschatological/thanatological corruption (Hades, Gehenna, Sheol, resurrection hope — assigned Theological/Divine-Human or Spiritual/God-ward). Session B should map how the moral and eschatological uses of corruption relate — do they share a common inner-being logic or are they genuinely distinct?

033-courage.finding: cha.zaq (H2388G) group 4923-002 ('Be strong and courageous' grounded in divine presence) is the paradigmatic biblical courage formula: courage is not self-generated but is a response to and product of divine accompaniment. The command and the promise of presence are structurally inseparable. Session B should examine cha.zaq's courage vocabulary as the primary inner-being theological claim of Reg 33: courage as relational-received-from-God, not as an autonomous character virtue.

034-covenant.finding: Three groups within the covenant registry show the new covenant as inner transformation (765-003, 766-002, 777-001 — law written on heart, new covenant through Spirit, new self/creation in Christ). Session B should examine the covenant registry's inner transformation dimension: how the trajectory from the Sinai covenant (obedience required externally, inner-being orientation demanded) to the new covenant (transformation from within, law on heart) is encoded in the Dimension Review data and what this discloses about the inner person as the site of covenantal fulfilment.

035-covetousness.finding: Reg 35 contains two analytically distinct sub-clusters: (1) willing-spirit/eager-disposition (prothumia, prothumos, prothumōs, euthumeō, euthumos, athumeō) and (2) covetous-desire proper (pleonexia). Session B should treat these as distinct inner-being phenomena requiring separate analytical attention.

039-debauchery.finding: Group 4986-001 in debauchery (#39, C12) contains 57 verses of salvation vocabulary (sōzō/sōtēria), anchored at Eph 2:8 ('by grace you have been saved through faith'). Related verses span the NT salvation corpus: Mat 10:22, 19:25, 24:13, Mar 16:16, Luk 8:12, Joh 3:17, Act 2:21, 4:12, and more. This group is structurally unexpected in the debauchery registry but was placed there on the basis of the verse evidence — salvation vocabulary appears in debauchery-adjacent contexts. Session B analyst should assess the connection: is salvation specifically named as the remedy for debauchery/licentiousness in these contexts, does the term appear because the debauchery file included a broad NT lexical sweep, or is there a distinct theological pattern linking salvation and debauchery vocabulary in Scripture? The answer has implications for how the inner-being dimension of salvation is characterised within this registry. This is also a Session D synthesis candidate — the relationship between salvation and debauchery vocabulary across the programme.

Group 779-001 (asōtia — debauchery) is explicitly contrasted with Spirit-filling in Eph 5:18: 'do not be drunk with wine, in which is debauchery, but be filled with the Spirit.' This creates a fundamental inner-being polarity: disordered inner filling (debauchery) vs. divine inner filling (Spirit). Session B should map this antithesis as a structural inner-being pair and examine whether it reflects a broader pattern in the cluster between defilement and the Spirit's filling/cleansing.

042-delight.finding: The delight vocabulary spans every dimension of the inner being. This breadth suggests delight is not a single category but a family of engagements organised around the axis of what the inner person is oriented toward. Session B analysis should map this orientation axis as the structuring principle of the registry.

H2654A (cha.phets) and H2654B (cha.phats) produce parallel three-group structures with equivalent classifications. Their functional equivalence across the corpus is a lexical data point for Session B — whether the two forms represent a single semantic range or carry distinguishable nuances.

Group 3090-001 (hēdonē — pleasure as the competing inner orientation, desires that war within) occupies a significant counter-position to the positive delight vocabulary. Pleasure as the adversary of the will toward God is a distinctive NT inner-being concept. Session B should examine the hēdonē / delight tension as a structural feature of the inner life.

043-desire.finding: Registry 43 (desire) shows a consistent divine/human register split: divine desire, will, and longing = Theological/Divine-Human; human desire and will = Volitional/Will or desiderative. These two registers appear throughout the vocabulary. Session B should read them in parallel and assess how Scripture's account of divine desire relates to human desire.

Misdirected desire dominates a large segment of Registry 43 — 10+ groups (464-001, 469-001, 480-001, 481-001, 481-003, 492-003, 497-001, 498-001, 504-001, 505-001) name desire as moral disorder. Session B should examine whether Scripture treats desire's moral valence as its primary theological concern, and how the disordered and rightly-ordered forms relate.

Group 513-001 names the divided inner will — the person wills good but does what they do not will (Romans 7 pattern). This is analytically foundational for understanding desire's relationship to action. Session B should give this sustained attention as a key inner-being mechanism.

044-despair.finding: The la/lo negation terms (H3809, H3808) build inner-being content through naming what is absent, refused, or withheld. The word does not carry inner-being content directly — it reveals inner states by negating them. Session B should examine this structural inversion as a distinctive biblical rhetoric for naming despair, faith-failure, and inner collapse.

046-devotion.finding: Registry 46 (devotion) centres on haplotēs/haplous — singleness, undivided-ness, wholeness of inner orientation. The primary analytical claim is that devotion is not primarily an emotional state (fervour) but a structural condition of the inner being: the undivided attention of the whole person toward God. The distraction group (5034-001) names the failure mode: the inner being pulled and scattered. Session B should examine singleness-of-heart as a central inner-being category distinct from both the zeal vocabulary of C07 and the mere performance of worship.

050-disobedience.finding: Reg 50 shows disobedience operating across three distinct inner-being dimensions in only 4 groups: Character/Disposition (defining orientation), Spiritual/God-ward (self-exclusion from divine rest), and Moral/Conscience (covenantal consequence). Session B should examine whether disobedience is primarily a dispositional, relational, or moral-act category in Scripture.

051-distress.finding: H7379 (riv, strife/contention) has 2 healthy active groups in distress (#51): 234-001 (strife/contention, 19 verses) and 234-002 (pleading one's cause, 26 verses). A third group was created for the indictment/controversy sense — God's legal case against his people in the prophetic tradition — but was never populated with verse_context records and has been delete_flagged. Session B analyst should assess whether this prophetic lawsuit dimension of H7379 belongs within distress and whether the verse evidence warrants a third group. Candidate verses would be found in prophetic lawsuit texts (Isaiah, Jeremiah, Micah) where H7379 names God's formal contention with Israel.

Group 235-002 (H7451A ra, harmful spirit afflicting Saul): assigned Theological/Divine-Human. This group sits on the boundary between demonological, psychological, and theological dimensions — the harmful spirit is theologically constituted (sent by God per 1 Sam 16) but produces what functions as an inner-psychological affliction. The existing dimension vocabulary may be inadequate to name this precisely. Session B should examine the verse evidence and consider whether a note on the vocabulary gap is warranted.

Registry 51 (distress) contains a significant cluster of outlier groups arriving via XREF terms that are outside the suffering domain: 242-001 (meditation/contemplation, Cognitive/Mind), 3600-001 (divine forming of the inner person, Theological/Divine-Human), 5129-002 (opened ear as inner receptivity, Spiritual/God-ward), 5168-001 (embrace as expression of fulfilment, Affective/Emotional), 5124-001 and 5126-001 (amazement/astonishment). These should be treated as peripheral in Session B analysis of Registry 51 and may warrant a vocabulary or cluster note.

052-division.finding: The Greek division vocabulary in the division registry (schizō G4977, schisma G4978, merizō G3307, diamerizō G1266, meris G3310, meros G3313) shows a differentiated inner-being landscape: schisma/schizō name communal fracture over identity evaluation; merizō names internal volitional division; diamerizō names interpersonal fracture from ultimate allegiance; meris/meros name relational belonging and volitional orientation toward Christ. Session B should examine these terms in relation to each other and to the Hebrew portion vocabulary (chelek, cheleq, chalaq).

Five groups in the division registry are assigned Divine-Human Correspondence (2962-002, 5274-002, 5280-001, 824-001, 828-001). This is a significant concentration. The portion/lot vocabulary (chelek, chalaq, meros, meris) consistently operates in divine-human correspondence mode — God allots, human receives, inner states arise in response. Session B should examine whether this correspondence structure is intrinsic to the portion concept or a feature of the particular biblical corpus.

Mark 1:10 (heavens torn open at Jesus's baptism) appears as a related verse in group 5272-001 (schizō). This is a cosmic/physical use of schizō with no inner-being subject in the term itself. Its presence in the inner-being corpus may reflect an over-broad Verse Context classification. Session B should assess whether the heavens-torn-open image carries inner-being significance through its context (divine approval, identity revelation) or whether it represents a classification gap.

056-envy.finding: Reg 56 (envy) holds the full positive-negative dual register of the zeal/jealousy vocabulary. The same inner passion (consuming zealous desire) appears as: (a) destructive human jealousy/envy (Affective/Emotional, Moral/Conscience), (b) positive human zeal for God (Spiritual/God-ward), (c) divine jealousy as exclusive covenantal claim (Theological/Divine-Human). The inner capacity is identical; direction of passion determines valence. Session B should map this tripartite structure and ask what makes the same inner passion sanctified or destructive.

057-evil.finding: Registry 57 (evil) is the largest in the programme (46 groups) and functions partly as a container for inner-being framing particles (kol H3605, mi H4310, na H4994, zamam H2161). Session B should assess whether this breadth is analytically productive for the evil analysis or whether the particle-generated groups obscure the core evil vocabulary (poneros, kakia, adikia, etc.).

Group 851-004 ('the impossibility of inner self-knowledge — the heart's moral corruption exceeds human capacity to understand') is a theologically significant statement about the opacity of the heart to itself. Session B should treat this as a key inner-being anthropological claim and examine its relationship to groups in other registries (e.g. conscience, self-knowledge, moral awareness).

058-experience.finding: Group 858-003 (ra.ah — seeing triggering inner response) captures a structural principle: the visual/perceptual act as a trigger mechanism for inner emotional and volitional activation. The group names a spectrum from desire to anger. Session B should examine whether ra.ah's role as inner-state activator is a consistent structural function across the Scripture corpus — specifically whether sight as inner trigger constitutes a distinct inner-being mechanism worth tracking in the programme vocabulary. The Gen 3:6 anchor (the Fall) and Job 32:5 (Elihu's anger) show this mechanism operating in both moral failure and righteous indignation contexts.

Group 858-010 has a SIGNIFICANT anchor verse mismatch confirmed on per-verse assessment. AV-1 (Gen 1:31 — God saw creation as very good) does not ground the description's claims about divine seeing of affliction/pride producing compassion/judgement. AV-2 (Gen 9:22 — Ham seeing Noah's nakedness) is a morally charged human seeing, not divine. The description's content appears entirely drawn from other verses in the group not represented in the anchor set. The current dimension assignment (Theological/Divine-Human, dominant subject NONE) has low confidence. Session B must: (a) review the full verse list for this group to verify whether the description is valid for the wider group; (b) assess whether Gen 1:31 and Gen 9:22 are correctly classified in this group or should be reclassified; (c) consider whether the anchor verse should be re-designated to a verse that actually grounds the description.

059-faith.finding: The faith registry contains two analytically distinct inner-being poles: faith/faithfulness as inner orientation toward God (trust, reliance, integrity), and unfaithfulness/treachery as the inner act of betrayal (maal, bagad, bogedet). Session B should treat these as mirror-image inner-being categories — what it means for the inner person to be faithful vs faithless.

G4102G pistis (faith) [860-001] and G4100 pisteuō (believing) [866-001] represent the noun form (faith as disposition) and verb form (believing as act) of the same inner-being phenomenon. Session B must analyse these together to characterise how Scripture frames trust-reliance as both a stable disposition and an active inner act.

060-faithfulness.finding: Three groups (872-001, 872-002, 872-007) in the faithfulness registry describe divine action — covenantal giving, compassionate giving, and eschatological transformation. The faithfulness registry operates significantly in the Theological/Divine-Human register, not primarily as human character description. Session B should map this divine-human asymmetry and assess whether 'faithfulness' as a registry label adequately captures the term natan's actual inner-being range.

061-fear.finding: fobeō (G5399) shows a tripartite inner-being structure: (1) theophanic/miraculous fear leading to worship (Spiritual/God-ward), (2) reverential life-shaping fear of God (Spiritual/God-ward), (3) practical self-interested fear before humans or consequences (Affective/Emotional). This tripartite structure is the most analytically important pattern in Registry 61 and should be the organising frame for Session B analysis.

ya.re (H3372G) groups add the divine 'Fear not' command (298-001) as a structuring element — showing the divine address to inner fear as a distinct inner-being category. The reassurance pattern (divine declaration of presence + commanded displacement of fear) is significant for understanding how the inner-being state of fear is meant to be transformed.

064-forgiveness.finding: Luk 7:47 (anchor for group 5377-001) establishes an explicit inner-being grammar of forgiveness: the quantity of forgiveness received determines the intensity of the inner orientation of love produced — "she loved much because she was forgiven much; he who is forgiven little loves little." This is not merely a motivational claim but an inner-being causal chain: divine forgiveness (vertical release) → inner love-orientation (relational disposition). Session B for Reg 64 should examine whether this causal chain — forgiveness received → love orientation produced — is a recurring inner-being pattern across the forgiveness corpus, and how it relates to the grace-supplication chain already identified in Reg 68 (grace given → supplication produced, from DIM-068 Session C data). The hypothesis: forgiveness and grace both operate as inner-being generators that produce specific downstream inner orientations (love and supplication respectively).

065-generosity.finding: agathos (G0018) groups in Reg 65 frame the inner being's relationship to goodness in four registers: (1) goodness as character source (good heart — Character/Disposition, 881-002), (2) goodness as moral command (do good — Moral/Conscience, 881-003), (3) goodness as inner conflict (want but cannot — Volitional/Will, 881-005), (4) goodness as divine attribute (only God is good — Theological/Divine-Human, 881-001). Group 881-005 is analytically most significant — the gap between inner desire for good and capacity to perform it is the heart of Pauline anthropology. Session B should examine how these four registers of goodness interact and what they reveal about the inner being's relationship to moral reality.

066-gentleness.finding: a.nah (H6031B) groups show affliction operating as an inner-being shaper in three modes: (1) affliction by others (1923-001 — experienced suffering), (2) affliction by God (1923-002 — purposive divine formation), (3) voluntary self-affliction (1923-003 — chosen submission). The same term covers all three, suggesting that the gentleness/meekness registry understands affliction as the formative mechanism of inner character. Session B should examine how gentleness is structurally connected to the affliction vocabulary — whether biblical gentleness is primarily the fruit of endured humbling.

067-goodness.finding: The tov word family spans Theological/Divine-Human (divine goodness), Moral/Conscience (human moral character), Spiritual/God-ward (experiential good), and Character/Disposition (Spirit-fruit) — a very wide dimensional range for one term. Session B should analyse whether these are genuinely distinct inner-being phenomena or expressions of a unified category of 'goodness' that the English vocabulary separates but Hebrew integrates.

068-grace.finding: Group 890-001 (ta.cha.nun, supplication) — Dan 9:3 anchor shows earnest supplication enacted somatically: fasting, sackcloth, ashes. The body enacts what the inner person is doing — the whole person brought into alignment with the inner posture of plea. Session B should examine the somatic signature of grace-supplication: whether the body-posture vocabulary of petition (prostration, fasting, weeping, raised hands) encodes inner-being content about the Dependence/Creatureliness orientation, and how this connects to the grace registry's inner-being picture. Zec 12:10 (shared anchor with 889-001) shows that the capacity for earnest supplication is itself grace-given — the Spirit of grace produces the plea. This is the inner-being grammar of grace-response: grace first, supplication as grace-enabled response.

069-gratitude.finding: All three gratitude terms converge on Character/Disposition — gratitude is not a transient affective response but a stable inner reorientation of the whole person. The absence of gratitude as a diagnostic marker of inner corruption (5480-001) is a particularly significant finding. Session B should examine gratitude as a diagnostic indicator of inner-being health.

070-greed.finding: G0726 harpazō appears in two groups with contrasting inner-being engagements: [5493-001] predatory violation of inner content (Spiritual/God-ward) and [5493-002] divine protective snatching (Theological/Divine-Human). Session B should examine whether these two uses require distinct analytical treatment or represent a unified category of inner-being vulnerability and security.

072-groaning.finding: Group 899-001 (G4726 stenagmos, the Spirit's inarticulate groaning in Romans 8): assigned Spiritual/God-ward. This is a pneumatological dimension unique in the C05 cluster — the Spirit interceding within the inner being through groaning that is beyond the person's own capacity for articulation. Session B should examine this group as a distinctive case of the inner being as the locus of the Spirit's intercessory activity.

073-guilt.finding: The guilt registry (Reg 73) shows Spiritual/God-ward as the dominant dimension — sin and guilt are primarily framed in Scripture as relational conditions before God rather than psychological states. Session B analysis should proceed from this orientation: guilt is fundamentally a God-ward condition, with Moral/Conscience and Affective/Emotional dimensions secondary.

G4893 suneidesis in Reg 73 shows a four-dimensional conscience profile: (1) moral witness [53-001], (2) vulnerability [53-002], (3) interior foundation [53-003], (4) object of purification [53-004]. Session B should analyse these four functions as a coherent inner-being anatomy of conscience.

077-honesty.finding: Group 911-002 (tsedeqah) is the only group in C10 explicitly engaging imputed righteousness — moral standing credited to the person by God. Session B should examine the theological significance of credited moral standing as a distinct inner-being category, and how it relates to the possessed moral character described in adjacent groups.

078-hope.finding: Hope in Registry 78 divides into three distinct modes: (a) hope as orientation or act directed toward God (Spiritual/God-ward — 30 groups across cluster); (b) hope as a constitutive inner quality or capacity of the person (Character/Disposition — groups 385-001, 401-002, 402-001, 968-001); (c) hope's absence, collapse, or disappointment (Affective/Emotional). Session B should assess whether these are three aspects of one dimension or three distinct inner-being engagements.

080-humility.finding: Registry 80 (humility) shows the biblical vocabulary of humility operating primarily as Character/Disposition (8 groups) with significant Theological/Divine-Human presence (4 groups). Humility is understood primarily as a character quality while simultaneously being the object of divine regard and the fruit of divine action (God humbling the proud). Group 57-002 (false humility) is analytically significant — the outward performance of abasement without genuine inner transformation. Session B should examine whether biblical humility is something the person cultivates, something God works in the person, or whether these are two aspects of the same inner reality, and how false humility is identified.

086-impurity.finding: Group 5574-001 (akathartos — unclean spirit) presents the unclean spirit as an inner occupant whose presence produces inner captivity and whose expulsion by Christ restores inner freedom. Session B should examine this as a model of inner-being occupation and liberation — the inner person as the site of spiritual occupancy — and how this relates to the programme's understanding of the inner being's relationship to spiritual forces.

089-iniquity.finding: Groups 926-001 and 926-002 (ani H6041 — afflicted person) describe the inner experience of the victim of injustice — material need, oppression, and dependence on God for vindication. This is a perspective distinct from the perpetrator-focused groups elsewhere in C11. Session B should examine how the inner experience of being wronged relates to the cluster's primary focus on moral failure as an inner condition.

090-innocence.finding: The functional presentational particles (hen H2005, hinneh H2009, hennah H2008) classified into the innocence registry produce a very wide group range — covering confession, theophany, moral verdict, wisdom reflection, and emotional experience. The innocence registry functions as a container for inner-being disclosure framing. Session B should assess whether this breadth is analytically productive or whether the verse context classifications for these particles should be re-examined.

092-integrity.finding: Group 935-001 (aftharsia — incorruptibility) introduces the eschatological transformation of the person at resurrection — a dimension not well captured by existing vocabulary labels. Assigned Theological/Divine-Human as best available fit. Session B should address whether incorruptibility belongs in the integrity domain or warrants separate treatment, and how eschatological constitution of the person relates to present moral character.

094-intercession.finding: [5696-001] names the Spirit's intercession within the person in wordless groanings — the intersection of the Spirit's inner work and the human inner-being in weakness. Session B should examine this group in relation to the prayer vocabulary in Reg 122, as the Spirit's intercession frames what human prayer is when language fails.

097-joy.finding: After dimension correction, 27 of 40 joy groups align with Spiritual/God-ward. Joy in Scripture is not primarily a free-floating affective state but is characteristically grounded in and directed toward God, his salvation, his presence, and his word. Session B should foreground this as the dominant finding of the registry.

Three groups name joy that persists through or is intensified by suffering: 364-001 (salad — defiant exultation), 381-002 (chairo — paradoxical joy in tribulation), and 3859-001 in Registry 42 (hēdista — most gladly in weakness). This convergent cross-term pattern names a distinct inner-being mode. Session B should examine joy-through-suffering as a structural feature rather than an anomaly.

Group 378-001 (chara — abiding inner state) is assigned Character/Disposition rather than Affective/Emotional because it names a stable characterising inner quality. This parallels gratitude in Registry 69. Both registries produce at least one group where the inner state is stable and dispositional rather than episodic. Session B should examine whether there is a category of joy-as-character-quality distinct from joy-as-affective-experience.

098-justice.finding: H4941G mish.pat (Reg 98) and H4941H mish.pat (Reg 24) are the same Hebrew term across two registries: five groups in Reg 98 and three groups in Reg 24 constitute the full inner-being profile. Session B must read both registries' mish.pat groups together for a complete analysis.

The dikaios/dikaiosunē/dikaiōsis/dikaioō family (G1342, G1343, G1344, G1347) shows a consistent tension: righteousness as divine act/attribute (Theological/Divine-Human) vs righteousness as human inner quality (Moral/Conscience). This is one of the most analytically significant tensions in the programme. Session B must examine this carefully.

H6664G tsedeq and H6663 tsadeq produce four distinct groups: (1) human inner quality [942-001], (2) divine attribute [942-002], (3) pursued inner orientation [942-003], (4) moral basis for governance [942-004]. This fourfold pattern in a single Hebrew root is a major Session B analytical focus.

103-love.finding: The love registry spans the broadest relational vocabulary in the programme — agapē, phileō, chesed, and related terms — encompassing God's love for humanity (GOD dominant), human love toward God, mutual love among persons, and disordered love. Session B should map the full spectrum of love's inner-being architecture: what distinguishes the different forms, how they interrelate, and what the coexistence of eros/philia/agapē in the same registry reveals about the inner-being's capacity for love.

Group 535-001 (ye.di.dut — beloved of Gods soul, Reg 103) is anchored by Jer 12:7: I have given the beloved of my soul into the hands of her enemies. The phrase ye.di.dut nap.shi (beloved of my soul/nephesh) attributes an intimate inner-being category to God — God has a nephesh, and the beloved relationship is located there. This raises a significant inner-being question for Session B of Reg 103: does the love vocabulary of Reg 103 contain a sub-pattern of terms that attribute inner-being states to God with the same vocabulary used for human inner-being states? The terms ye.di.dut (beloved of my soul), ra.cha.mim (my heart yearns — Jer 31:20 in group 551-001), and the dod vocabulary (God as lover/beloved in Song and prophets) may all encode a divine inner-being reality using the same vocabulary as the human inner person. Session B should investigate whether Reg 103 contains a theological claim about divine inner-being experience, and how this connects to the programmes governing question about the inner characteristics of the human person.

108-meditation.finding: Higgayon (H1902H) operates as a directionally-determined cognitive act — the same reflective faculty deployed in devout meditation toward God (975-001) and in hostile scheming against others (5883-001). The act is morally neutral in itself; direction determines its character. This is Sub-pattern A of the directionally-determined inner faculty pattern identified across C02.

111-mercy.finding: The atonement/propitiation vocabulary (kipper, kaphar, kapporet, hilasmos, hilastērios) within the mercy registry produces a cluster of Transformation/GOD groups where moral standing is transformed through atoning acts. Session B should examine the relationship between mercy as divine inner disposition (Relational Disposition/GOD) and atonement as its structural mechanism — how God's inner relational orientation of mercy is expressed in and through transformative acts that alter the person's standing.

112-mind.finding: Memory in biblical vocabulary is an active, covenantal, morally significant act — not passive cognitive retrieval. God's remembering produces redemptive action; Israel's commanded remembering constitutes covenant identity; forgetting is moral defection.

The mind is a moral organ before it is an intellectual one. It can be debased, blinded, corrupted, renewed, and transferred (mind of Christ). Romans 12:2 presents renewal of the nous as the locus of personal transformation.

The ye.tser (formed inclination) is the OT anthropology's closest approach to a foundational moral disposition. Gen 6:5 and 8:21 establish it as bent toward evil from youth — the dispositional substrate generating particular thoughts and plans.

The cha.shav root documents that the same mental faculty produces artistic design, moral reasoning, and malicious plotting — there is no separate creative vs rational faculty in Hebrew anthropology. God's planning uses the same root as wicked scheming.

Fronēma (the set mindset) in Romans 8:5-7 is the determinative factor for life or death, peace or enmity with God — making the mind's fundamental orientation soteriologically significant, not merely morally relevant.

Suneidēsis (conscience) is the NT's distinctive contribution with no direct Hebrew equivalent. The conscience can be weak, clear, seared, or purified (Heb 9:14), and functions as a bridge between inner life and relational/covenantal domains.

Deu 4:9 functionally links the sha.mar attentiveness cluster with the za.khar memory cluster: vigilant attentiveness is the means by which memory is maintained; forgetting is the product of failed attentiveness.

The 1 Cor 14:14-15 nous/pneuma distinction is the sharpest NT terminological boundary between mind-level and spirit-level inner operation — directly relevant to the Framework B spirit-soul-body boundary investigation.

Double-mindedness (dipsuchos/se.eph) documents inner division as a cross-testament pathology. James presents inner unity as a condition for receiving from God and for effective action; Ps 119:113 makes it a moral stance.

The Hebrews new covenant quotation (Heb 8:10; 10:16) names both dianoia (mind) and kardia (heart) as sites of internalized law — anticipating the structural relationship between Registries 112 and 183 for Session D investigation.

sha.mar family (H8104) spans four dimensions across Registry 112: Volitional/Will (G, covenantal obedience), Theological/Divine-Human (H, God's guarding), Moral/Conscience (H subset self-guarding; I attentive observation), Character/Disposition (J, vigilance). Session B: map the full sha.mar semantic range across all inner-being functions.

se.eph (4198-001, H5588) and dipsuchos (1398-001, G1374) form an OT/NT pair documenting inner division as a cross-testament moral pathology. Both assigned Moral/Conscience. Session B: analyse together as witnesses to the same inner-being phenomenon — the split allegiance that makes the person unstable before God.

ye.tser (936-001/936-002, H3336) spans Moral/Conscience (formed moral inclination toward evil or good) and Theological/Divine-Human (creaturely formation known and addressed by God). The OT's closest approach to a foundational moral disposition is simultaneously a moral reality and a creaturely-relational one. Session B: the ye.tser as the dispositional substrate of moral life.

114-obedience.finding: Reg 114 contains the counter-term to disobedience: [1020-002] names Christ's obedience as the inner act that reverses Adam's. Session B should examine the obedience/disobedience polarity across Reg 114 and Reg 50 (C13) as a structurally significant inner-being opposition.

115-passion.finding: Registry 115 (passion) contributes zero desiderative groups in Phase C. Group 1023-001 names defiling passion as moral drive; 5948-001 names moral defilement; 1432-001 (deferred) names patient attentiveness. Session B should clarify what the passion registry actually covers in its term set and whether the English label accurately reflects the Hebrew/Greek semantic range.

116-patience.finding: Registry 116 (patience) shows a structural split: patience as human character quality (Character/Disposition, 7 groups) and patience as divine attribute (Theological/Divine-Human, 3 groups — 32-002, 33-002, 83-001). The divine patience groups show God's own inner disposition of forbearance modelled as the archetype of what believers are commanded to embody. Session B should examine whether biblical patience is explicitly presented as imitation of the divine disposition, and whether the vocabulary signals this connection.

117-peace.finding: Peace (#117) carries 15 terms with status=extracted_theological_anchor (818 active verses). These are divine names, titles, and eternal/hosts vocabulary — YHWH-Shalom, LORD of Hosts (H6635A/B, 292 verses), El Shaddai (H7706, 48 verses), Adonai (H0136, 187 verses), El Olam (H5769G/H, 273 verses), El Elyon (H5943/H5946, 14 verses), YHWH-Nissi, YHWH-Tsidkenu, YHWH-Tsidkenu (H6664H, 2 verses), sabaoth (G4519, 2 verses). Deliberately excluded from VCB — no verse_context records exist for these terms. Their active verses remain unflagged by design. Session B DataPrep must include these terms as theological frame context for the peace word analysis. Verse records are accessible via wa_verse_records joined through wa_term_inventory. See word_registry.notes for full term list.

The peace registry's inner-being content is unusually wide — spanning the felt inner state of rest/peace (Emotion — Positive), the inner posture of quietness before God (Moral Character/Relational Disposition), the divine giving of rest (Transformation/GOD), and God's own inner states of rest, restraint, and jealousy. Session B should examine what shalom names as an inner-being reality: is it primarily a felt state, a relational condition, a divine gift, or an eschatological reality — and how do these dimensions interrelate?

120-perverseness.finding: The ha.phakh word family (5949 series, 4 groups) encompasses both corrupting perversion and renewing transformation — overturn in either direction. Group 5949-001 assigns Spiritual/God-ward for the mixed group; 5949-002 assigns Affective/Emotional for affective transformation; 5949-003 confirms Theological/Divine-Human for divine inner change. Session B should examine whether perversion and transformation are genuinely polar uses of the same root, and what this reveals about the inner-being concept of transformation in Scripture.

121-praise.finding: The doxa/glory sub-cluster ([1047-001] through [1047-004]) in Reg 121 shows glory operating as: (1) supreme inner orientation target, (2) transforming agent through beholding, (3) doxological purpose, (4) eschatological hope. Session B should analyse this fourfold glory pattern as a significant inner-being framework within the praise domain.

122-prayer.finding: The prayer registry shows a nearly uniform Spiritual/God-ward dimension profile. Session B should examine what distinguishes prayer from other Spiritual/God-ward orientations (praise, worship, seeking, faith) — what is specifically constitutive of prayer as an inner act: the bringing of the will and need before God.

123-pride.finding: Registry 123 (pride) carries the most analytically complex dual-register in the cluster: human pride and divine majesty share the same vocabulary roots (ga.ah, ga.on, rum, ra.mam). The kauchaomai (26-001/002/003) groups make the structure explicit: legitimate boasting is directed toward God (Spiritual/God-ward); illegitimate boasting turns inward or toward self (Character/Disposition). The biblical claim is that pride is the human attempt to claim for oneself what belongs to God's majesty alone. Session B should examine the pride/majesty dual-register as a structured theological claim about the relationship between divine transcendence and human self-assertion.

Group 26-003 (Paul's paradoxical boasting in weakness — sustained inner act of inverting normal pride to reveal divine power) is analytically distinctive. This is not the absence of pride but its structural transformation: weakness embraced as the vehicle through which God's power is displayed. This group bridges C09 (pride/boasting) and C08 (Reg 170 weakness). Session B should examine this as a distinct inner-being posture: the voluntary inversion of the pride-orientation as itself a form of God-ward inner act.

124-prophecy.finding: Registry 124 (prophecy) shows a consistent pattern of Agency/Power (Spirit-enabled capacity exercised) alongside Relational Disposition (inner orientation toward God or his word). Session B should examine the relationship between inner capacity and inner orientation in the prophetic person: is prophetic capacity a dimension in its own right, or is it always constituted by and in service of a prior relational orientation toward God?

125-purity.finding: The tahor/taher word family in the purity registry spans ritual cleansing (external covenantal state), moral purification (inner), and divine restorative action. Session B should map how the ritual and moral dimensions relate — and whether the programme treats purity primarily as an inner character quality or as a covenantal status granted and maintained through prescribed practice.

127-reasoning.finding: Dialogismos (G1261) displays the full range of its directionally-determined character in C02: 1081-001 names corrupt inner reasoning originating in the heart (Moral/Conscience) and 1081-002 names the mind's legitimate deliberation on contested matters (Cognitive/Mind). Same root, same faculty, opposite moral orientation. Session B should treat dialogismos as a directionally-determined term whose dimension shifts with context.

128-rebellion.finding: H5493H (sur, to turn aside/depart) in rebellion (#128) has 2 healthy active groups: 6077-001 (departure of God's Spirit, 5 verses) and 6077-003 (departure of inner strength, 3 verses). A third group (6077-002, departure from God) had 4 verse_context records that were all subsequently delete_flagged for unknown reasons. The 4 orphaned verses are: Deu 4:9 (warning not to turn aside from what was seen), Deu 7:4 (intermarriage turning sons from following God), 2Ki 3:3 (not departing from the sin of Jeroboam), Num 16:26 (command to depart from tents of Korah). The group shell has been delete_flagged. Session B analyst should review whether any of these 4 verses meet the inner-being filter for rebellion and should be reassigned to 6077-001 or 6077-003, or whether a distinct apostasy group is warranted.

The sur (to turn aside) sub-gloss groups in Reg 128 show a consistent pattern: turning aside can be from God (6076-001 — Spiritual/God-ward negative), from idols toward God (6075-001 — Spiritual/God-ward positive), or toward God (6076-003 — Spiritual/God-ward positive). The same volitional act (turning) is the mechanism for both rebellion and repentance — only the direction differs. Session B should examine whether the turning vocabulary illuminates rebellion as fundamentally a directional problem of the inner being, and what this implies for understanding repentance as the reversal of the same inner movement.

Group 6077-002 in Reg 128 (the orphaned group from DIM-128-001) has anchor=0 and related=0. The four orphaned verses are: Deu 4:9 (warning not to turn aside from what was seen), Deu 7:4 (intermarriage turning sons from following God), 2Ki 3:3 (not departing from the sin of Jeroboam), Num 16:26 (command to depart from tents of Korah). Session B analyst should review whether any meet the inner-being filter for rebellion and should be reassigned to 6077-001 or 6077-003, or whether a distinct apostasy group is warranted. (Continuing DIM-128-001 instruction.)

131-rejection.finding: apobolē (1094-001, Rom 11:15) carries one of the highest concentrations of theological density in the programme — rejection, reconciliation, and life from the dead in a single verse. The group is assigned Divine-Human Correspondence with GOD as dominant subject. Session B should examine the inner-being dynamic of Israel's covenantal rejection: what the inner-relational experience of being cast off by God means for the human inner person, and how it relates to the redemptive correspondence that Paul draws.

apoballō (6115-001) covers only two NT verses (Heb 10:35 and Mark 10:50) — one warning against throwing away confidence, one depicting urgent casting aside of impediment toward Christ. These are inverse volitional acts: one guarding an inner disposition, one releasing an external obstacle. Session B should examine whether these two uses illuminate a single inner-being faculty (the will's relationship to what it holds or releases) or represent genuinely different phenomena.

135-repentance.finding: Registry 135 shows a consistent human-divine mirroring structure: nearly every human inner-being act (turning, relenting, comforting, recalling) has a divine counterpart in the same registry (God relenting, God comforting, God withholding wrath). Session B should analyse this pairing as a key structural feature of the repentance domain in Scripture.

140-seeking.finding: The seeking registry contains seeking vocabulary (darash, shachar) that is among the most intense inner-being orientation language in Scripture — the soul yearning for God. Session B should examine seeking as a specific form of Spiritual/God-ward orientation distinct from faith (trust-reliance) and worship (devotional service) — seeking names the active inner pursuit of encounter with God.

142-self-control.finding: Registry 142 (self-control) shows the self-governance vocabulary operating in three registers: (a) human self-control as character virtue (1106-001, 1113-001, 6203-001), (b) human volitional exercise of restraint (1107-001, 6208-001, 1109-001, 1111-001), (c) Spirit-gift of self-discipline as divine endowment (1112-001). The human effort and the divine gift coexist and the vocabulary does not sharply distinguish them. Session B should examine whether biblical self-control is primarily a virtue cultivated, a gift received, or an exercise of will — or whether all three are held in tension as aspects of the same inner-being governance.

146-shame.finding: Shame vocabulary in Registry 146 operates on two consistent axes: (a) inner shame as felt condition (affective, moral, spiritual) and (b) shame as socially-imposed or relationally-enacted condition (relational, theological). The same Hebrew/Greek words move between these axes. Session B should map how Scripture relates the inner experience of shame to its social construction and how these axes intersect in the biblical theology of honour/shame.

147-sin.finding: The al H0408 particle groups (1119 series, 9 groups) produce a cross-dimensional set spanning fear, anger, grief, rejoicing, trust, pride, concern, and cognitive orientation — the full range of inner-being states negated in divine or human address. Session B should assess whether these groups contribute meaningfully to the analysis of sin as an inner-being phenomenon or whether they represent general inner-being vocabulary included through verse context classification.

149-slander.finding: The slander registry contains three foot-related groups (6253-001: moral life direction; 6253-002: relational submission/devotion; 6256-001: teaching to walk) whose connection to slander is via the XREF lexical path rather than conceptual affinity. Session B should assess whether these groups contribute meaningfully to the slander analysis or whether they represent peripheral inner-being vocabulary that should be noted as structurally incidental.

155-submission.finding: The submission registry distinguishes two dimensions: relational submission to human authority ([1147-001], Relational/Social) and submission before God ([1148-001], Spiritual/God-ward). Session B should examine whether this distinction is sustained across the broader submission/surrender/yielding vocabulary in C14.

160-thought.finding: Registry 160 (thought) contains three instances of the directionally-determined inner faculty pattern: (1) re.a (H7454) — 3453-001 names thoughts known by God and God's thoughts precious to the psalmist, the same relational faculty operating from both sides of the divine-human boundary simultaneously (Sub-pattern C); (2) dialogismos (G1261) — 3409-001 names inner thoughts as site of spiritual warfare and accusation (Moral/Conscience) and 3410-001 names inner reckoning as the ground of faith (Cognitive/Mind) — same root, opposite orientation; (3) fronimos (G5429) pattern carries from C01: prudence and craftiness as the same perceptive faculty (Sub-pattern B). Session B must treat these as analytically distinct uses of the same terms.

163-trust.finding: The trust registry shows Character/Disposition as the dominant dimension — trust as a stable inner posture of reliance rather than primarily an act. Session B should examine the relationship between trust-as-posture (Character/Disposition) and faith-as-act (Volitional/Will in faith registry) — two complementary ways Scripture names the inner orientation toward God.

164-truthfulness.finding: The aletheia word family spans five distinct inner-being dimensions across 10 groups: Spiritual/God-ward, Volitional/Will, Character/Disposition, Theological/Divine-Human, Cognitive/Mind. No single dimension captures the full range. Session B should examine whether 'truthfulness' is the right registry label, and whether the term's biblical range is better described as 'truth-orientation' or another formulation that captures both the character quality and the transformational/pneumatological dimensions.

167-unity.finding: Psalm 133:1 ('how good and pleasant it is when brothers dwell in unity') appears as a related verse in group 1210-002 (gam — extension function) rather than in a unity-specific group. This is analytically notable: the programme's most direct unity statement appears under a grammatical particle's extension function. Session B should examine whether this reflects the Verse Context classification correctly placing gam as the owning term, or whether Ps 133:1 belongs under a different unity term.

The unity registry contains three groups for gam (H1571, 404 occurrences) with a very high set-aside rate (272 of 404). The three groups capture distinct inner-being functions: intensification (Ps 23:4 paradigm → Dependence/Creatureliness), extension (Joel 2:29 paradigm → Divine-Human Correspondence), and the Ecclesiastes vanity-verdict (Cognition). Session B should examine whether gam's assignment to the unity registry is semantically justified beyond the additive/inclusive surface connection, and whether the three groups together reveal a coherent inner-being contribution.

168-uprightness.finding: Group 1216-006 (ya.shar — 'God made man upright, but they have sought out many schemes', Eccl. 7:29) is the only group in C10 explicitly addressing the original moral constitution of the human person and its departure. Session B should treat this as a significant anthropological statement and examine its implications for understanding uprightness as a creational category within the inner-being framework.

170-weakness.finding: Registry 170 (weakness) is analytically anomalous within C09 (vice cluster). The weakness vocabulary in Paul shows a structured theological inversion: weakness is simultaneously a limitation (Affective/Emotional, 1219-001) and the privileged inner-being condition in which divine power is revealed (Theological/Divine-Human, 1219-002; 6639-003), God elects (Theological/Divine-Human, 6639-003), and Christ's pattern is enacted. Session B should examine weakness as a paradoxical inner-being category and ask whether it belongs analytically within C09 or whether it represents a distinct inner-being posture better addressed in relation to C08 (endurance) or C15 (faith/trust).

171-whoredom.finding: All seven whoredom groups are dominated by spiritual/covenantal whoredom — Israel's heart-departure toward foreign gods. The literal sexual dimension appears in only one group (1221-004). Session B should examine how the spiritual whoredom metaphor relates to the literal sexual meaning as inner-being phenomena, and whether covenantal faithfulness is the primary inner-being register of this vocabulary in Scripture.

173-will.finding: The will registry shows the widest dimension spread in C14, spanning Volitional/Will, Theological/Divine-Human, Affective/Emotional, Spiritual/God-ward, Character/Disposition, Cognitive/Mind, Identity/Selfhood, Moral/Conscience. Session B must begin with sub-cluster taxonomy: which groups name the volitional act itself, which name surrounding conditions, and which entered via functional/grammatical terms.

The halakh (to go/walk) groups ([1235-001] through [1235-006]) form a coherent inner-being sub-cluster: inner movement as metaphor for inner orientation. Session B should analyse this motif — the direction of inner movement as the register of spiritual orientation (toward God, toward idols, toward death).

174-wisdom.finding: Registry 174 (wisdom) contains multiple directionally-determined term pairs: (1) a.rum (H6175) and or.mah (H6195) — prudence as cognitive faculty vs. craftiness as moral orientation toward deception (Sub-pattern B); (2) sofos/sofia (G4680/G4678) — wisdom toward God (4458-001, Moral/Conscience character type) vs. wisdom turned inward as self-deception (4458-002, Moral/Conscience distortion); (3) cha.kham (H2449/H2450) — wisdom from fear of Lord (528-003, Spiritual/God-ward) vs. wisdom self-attributed (528-003 note). The cluster reveals that wisdom vocabulary in Scripture is structurally shaped by the direction of the soul's orientation. Session B should treat wisdom terms as directionally-sensitive throughout.

175-wonder.finding: Wonder vocabulary divides between the divine side (Theological/Divine-Human) and the human response (Affective/Emotional). Session B should examine wonder as the inner state at the interface between human capacity and divine transcendence — the point where the inner person's capacity to comprehend is exceeded by what God is and does.

176-worship.finding: The worship registry contains three distinct sub-clusters worth separate Session B attention: (1) proskuneō/shachah — prostration as the physical-inner act of submission/devotion; (2) avodah/latreia — service/ministry as vocation; (3) non-worship XREF vocabulary (pit, dog, work, bondage). Session B should focus primarily on (1) and (2) as the core worship inner-being domain.

178-wrath.finding: The cho.mah (wall, H2346G) groups in Reg 178 (6784-001 to 6784-006) represent an XREF pattern where an architectural term's inner-being applications cover: misplaced trust (false security replacing God-trust), self-mastery (undefended city), purity (chaste self-possession), divine protection, divine attentiveness, and prophetic resilience. Session B should assess whether these six wall-groups illuminate a coherent inner-being pattern about security vs. vulnerability and self-possession vs. dissolution, and how this relates to the wrath registry.

180-yielding.finding: The paradidōmi (G3860) cluster in Reg 180 ([6835-001] through [6835-012]) spans 12 distinct inner-being uses: betrayal, Christ's passion, voluntary self-giving, judicial delivering over, apostolic transmission, discipleship under persecution, stewardship, continual self-entrustment, self-abandonment to desire, divine delegation, discipline for restoration, obedience to tradition. Session B must treat these 12 groups as a structured taxonomy of 'giving over/handing on' as an inner-being category.

The dōron/didōmi gift-vocabulary sub-cluster in Reg 180 shows a consistent pattern: giving as the domain where inner character is most directly revealed — generosity, greed, love, indifference, dependence, and gratitude all surface through the giving action. Session B should examine giving as a primary inner-being diagnostic.

181-zeal.finding: Reg 181 (zeal/spoudē) is uniformly positive — lacking the negative jealousy/envy register entirely. Dimension profile: Character/Disposition (3), Spiritual/God-ward (1), Volitional/Will (1). This contrasts with Reg 56 zeal vocabulary (predominantly Affective/Emotional and Spiritual/God-ward, both positive and negative valence). Session B should analyse what distinguishes earnest diligence (spoudē, Reg 181) from consuming passion (zēlos, Reg 56) — whether vocabulary difference, semantic field distinction, or theological claim about godly zeal.

182-Soul.finding: Ne.phesh derives from the Hebrew word for throat — the passage through which breath and food move. The soul begins as somatic (throat, breath, hunger) and develops organically to denote the whole animated person, not by metaphorical extension but by the unity of breathing, desiring, and living.

The soul is not an immortal substance lodged within the body — it is the animated body itself. Lev 17:11 identifies the soul with the blood. H5315M (dead soul = corpse) confirms the soul does not vacate a container at death but ceases to animate its flesh. The biblical soul is not Cartesian.

The human soul shares its category (ne.phesh cha.yah — living creature) with animals (Gen 1:20,21,24). What distinguishes the human soul is not the noun but the act — the direct divine inbreathing of Gen 2:7. The soul is not uniquely human as a category but is uniquely constituted in the human.

The soul is constitutively appetitive — it is always reaching toward something. H5315L establishes this as the soul's fundamental structure. The critical question is always the object of craving: the soul that pants for God (Psa 42:1) and the soul that hungers for food (Deu 12:20) are using the same capacity.

The soul-life paradox of the NT: the soul that clings to itself loses itself; the soul that gives itself away finds itself. Mat 10:39 and its parallels structure the soul as both the thing most desperately sought (self-preservation) and the thing that must be released to be truly found. This is the transformation dynamic at the heart of the NT soul vocabulary.

G5591 psuchikos establishes the soul without the Spirit as a real condition — the natural creaturely mode of human existence. The soul is not evil in this mode, but incomplete. 1Co 15:45 establishes the eschatological trajectory: the psuchikon (Adamic) is raised as the pneumatikon (Christic). The soul is the beginning of the journey, not its end.

God employs soul-vocabulary for his own inner states: Exo 31:17 (God refreshed — na.phash), Isa 42:1 (God's soul delights), Jer 32:41 (God's whole-soul commitment). Soul-language is the available language for divine inner states analogous to human inner states — not asserting God has a creaturely soul but using soul vocabulary analogically for divine pathos.

The soul's self-address in Psa 42-43 (the psalmist speaks to his own soul, diagnoses it, gives it a counter-command) is the OT's most psychologically sophisticated move. The person who can observe their own soul from outside and command it is more whole, not less — this reflexive capacity is a distinctive feature of the soul vocabulary.

Heb 4:12 — the Word of God divides soul and spirit as a sword divides joints and marrow. The soul cannot reliably distinguish itself from the spirit by introspection alone. The soul-spirit distinction is real but requires divine speech to make it visible — it is not self-evident to the person.

The soul is constitutively relational: to God (Psa 42:1, 62:1, 63:1), to other souls (1Sa 18:1, Act 4:32), to its own orientation (dipsuchos — inner division). Soul-to-soul bonding (sumpsuchos, isopsuchos) documents genuine union of orientation between persons — community is possible when souls share direction.

ne.phesh groups 1381-008 (Servant's soul poured out in self-offering, Isa 53) and 1392-002 (psuchē life laid down in love, Christ for the sheep) together form the atonement-love axis in the soul registry. Both assigned Theological/Divine-Human. Session B: these are the programme's primary soul-and-atonement texts — connect to existing SD flags 182-F005 (Isa 53 atonement) and PH2-182-005 (eschatological soul-substitution).

Identity/Selfhood groups in the Soul registry (1385-001 human as living soul, 1391-001 psuchē as essential inner person, 1396-001/2 psuchikos as natural mode, 1400-001 apsuchos as negation) together define what the soul is constitutively. Session B: these form the ontological foundation of Registry 182 — the soul as a mode of being, not merely a function.

Soul (#182) carries 1 term with status=phase2_enrichment: H5317 (nophet/honey, 5 active verses). Pro 27:7 — one who is full loathes honey; to one who is hungry everything bitter is sweet — encodes the appetite dimension of nephesh through honey as archetype metaphor. Deliberately excluded from VCB — no verse_context records exist. Active verses remain unflagged by design. Decision carried forward from prior Session B. Session B DataPrep must include this term as contextual support for understanding the soul's appetitive dimension. Verses: Psa 19:10, Pro 5:3, Pro 24:13, Pro 27:7, Song 4:11.

183-heart.finding: qe.rev (H7130H) spans five groups across five dimensions: Theological/Divine-Human (God's indwelling in the midst), Moral/Conscience (seat of moral character; seat of hostile intent; location of false prophets' lies), Theological/Divine-Human (location of new covenant renewal). Session B: the inner parts as the site of both divine indwelling and moral corruption — a structural inner-being tension at the heart of the heart registry.

184-spirit.finding: pneuma (G4151G) spans seven groups covering seven distinct inner-being functions: Holy Spirit transforming presence, human spirit as experiential seat, spirit-flesh contrast, unclean spirit, spirit-characterised orientations, life-giving Spirit, spiritual gifts. This is the most dimensionally diverse single term in C01. Session B: the spirit vocabulary in NT is the analytical heart of Registry 184 — mapping its full range requires the complete seven-group reading.

Registry 184 has the highest Theological/Divine-Human density of any C01 registry: 17 of 37 groups (46%) assigned Theological/Divine-Human. Session B: the spirit vocabulary is inherently relational — the human spirit exists primarily in the context of God's Spirit. This distinguishes spirit from soul and heart in the C01 structural analysis.

186-gladness.finding: Ya.tav (H3190) produces three materially distinct dimension patterns: Affective/Emotional (merry heart), Moral/Conscience (moral goodness), and Affective/Emotional (approval/pleasure). This bridges the moral and affective dimensions within a single gladness/goodness term. Session B should examine whether the Hebrew gladness/goodness vocabulary reveals an integration of moral and affective categories that the programme's dimension vocabulary treats separately.

187-strength.finding: Group 7013-001 (a.yil, ram) carries a verse-level valuation in which obedience (H8085H sha.ma) and attentive listening (H7181 qashav) are declared greater than sacrifice — greater than the fat of rams (1 Sam 15:22). Neither term is an OWNER in Registry 187; their inner-being weight is present in the verse but unregistered in the programme. Session B for Registry 187 should explore: (1) what the comparative structure of 1 Sam 15:22 reveals about the hierarchy of inner-being orientations toward God — obedience and attentive listening as the higher register; (2) how this valuation relates to the strength/capacity cluster's broader understanding of inner-being agency before God; (3) the relationship between sha.ma and qashav as paired inner-being acts that together constitute the responsive orientation of the person toward God.

190-contempt.finding: Contempt vocabulary in Registry 190 operates at three levels: (a) the inner disposition of contempt as evaluative stance (Character/Disposition), (b) contempt as social-relational dynamic (Relational/Social), (c) contempt with theological weight directed at God, his word, or his servant (Moral/Conscience, Theological/Divine-Human). Session B should map how these three registers of contempt relate in the inner being.

191-doubt.finding: Group 1286-001 (H2963 ta.raph, tearing) was assigned Emotion — Negative / HUMAN to capture the human experience of violent inner states: wrath, predatory character, destructive anger, and the suffering of being torn. The same group contains significant GOD-dimension content — divine wrath and fierce resolve to fight for his people. Session B should study both dimensions: (1) the human inner-being experience of violent states and of being torn under judgment or hostile power; and (2) the divine inner-being reality of wrath and fierce protective resolve, exploring whether these are the same phenomenon viewed from different subjects or whether they name distinct inner-being realities.

192-comfort.finding: Comfort vocabulary splits cleanly: God-given comfort (Spiritual/God-ward) and community-given comfort (Relational/Social). Paraklēsis spans both dimensions across its three groups. Session B should examine the structural relationship between divine and human comfort — the data suggests divine comfort both enables and models the human comfort that flows from it.

194-blessing.finding: The blessing vocabulary reveals a mirror structure in Relational Disposition: group 1299-001 names God's inner disposition of love as the source of his blessing toward persons (GOD dominant); group 1299-002 names the human inner orientation of worship and praise as the act of blessing God (HUMAN dominant). Session B should examine this mutual relational structure — whether the blessing vocabulary discloses a covenantal reciprocity in inner-being orientation between God and persons, and what this reveals about the inner being as the site of covenant relationship.

201-image.finding: Three groups in the image registry (1359-001 tse.lem, 1360-001 de.mut, 1361-001 eikōn) all assign Divine-Human Correspondence to the imago Dei statement under three different terms. This convergence across Hebrew tse.lem, Hebrew de.mut, and Greek eikōn is analytically significant: all three terms independently arrive at the same dimension for the same verse (Gen 1:26-27). Session B should examine the imago Dei as the ontological foundation of Divine-Human Correspondence throughout the programme — whether the image-bearing of the human person is the root from which all other divine-human correspondences in the inner being flow.

Group 1361-002 (eikōn — 2 Cor 3:18, 'transformed into the same image from glory to glory') is assigned Transformation. This group is structurally adjacent to the three imago Dei groups but names a process rather than a state — the inner person being actively changed toward the divine image. Session B should examine the relationship between bearing the image (static ontological correspondence) and being transformed toward it (dynamic process) — these may be two aspects of a single inner-being reality that need to be held together.

204-name.finding: The name registry (Reg 204) shows a consistent Hebrew-Greek parallel structure: 1366-001/1367-001 (divine name as self-disclosure), 1366-002/1367-002 (human orientation through the divine name), 1366-003/1367-003 (name as moral character), 1366-004/1367-004 (naming as identity transformation). The same inner-being structure appears under both shem and onoma. Session B should examine whether this structural parallel reflects a unified understanding of naming as inner-being constitutive act across the Testaments, or whether there are genuine differences between the Hebrew and Greek registers.

206-vulnerability.finding: Genesis 3:10 (group 7414-001 — e.rom) is the foundational inner-being text for the vulnerability cluster: 'I was afraid, because I was naked, and I hid myself.' This encodes the primary inner-being sequence of moral failure: nakedness-awareness → fear → self-concealment. Session B should examine how this three-stage sequence (exposure of inner condition → negative emotional response → volitional self-concealment from God) structures the anthropology of inner vulnerability across the OT and NT. The contrast with Gen 2:25 (naked and not ashamed) establishes the pre-fall / post-fall inner-being contrast as a structural framework for the vulnerability cluster.

207-blindness (spiritual).finding: The darkness vocabulary (skotos, skotia, skoteinos, skotizō, skotoō) generates multiple distinct inner-being characteristics across its groups: identity condition (7432-001, 7433-001), moral domain (7432-002), concealment/cognition (7432-003, 7433-002), eschatological consequence (7432-004), whole-person darkening (7435-001), and active darkening of faculties (7434-001). This is a term family with unusually wide inner-being range. Session B should assess whether these groups reflect a coherent single inner-being phenomenon (darkness as multi-dimensional spiritual reality) or whether the groupings require further subdivision.

208-sloth.finding: Group 1374-001 (the sluggard whose desire craves but whose will refuses to act) names sloth's inner-being structure precisely: not the absence of desire but the uncoupling of desire from volitional action. This is the inner-being gap that defines sloth — desire without will. Session B should examine whether the sloth vocabulary in Reg 208 consistently identifies this desire/will gap, or whether sloth is also described as the absence of desire itself, and what this reveals about the relationship between desire, will, and action in the biblical anthropology.

210-deadness.finding: Group 7423-004 (H4194 ma.vet) contains two anchor verses in potential tension: Psa 6:5 (in death there is no remembrance of you; in Sheol who will give you praise — incapacity of the dead) and Psa 116:15 (Precious in the sight of the Lord is the death of his saints — God's relational valuation of the dying). The group description addresses the incapacity reading only. Session B should assess whether these two verses represent a coherent group or whether 7423-004 should be subdivided: one group naming the incapacity of the dead, one naming God's regard for the death of the faithful.

The deadness vocabulary (nekros, thanatos, ma.vet, mut) generates a consistent dual-register pattern: death as condition (Vitality/Existence) and death as moral/relational reality (Moral Character, Transformation, Relational Disposition). The same root terms name both the physical/existential fact of death and the spiritual/moral state it represents. Session B should map this dual-register explicitly — particularly the distinction between dead as condition you are in (nekros 1378-001, 1378-005; thanatos 7427-001) versus dead as act you perform or participate in (nekros 1378-002; nekroō 7422-001; thanatos 7427-003).

Group 7427-003 (G2288 thanatos) contains two anchor verses with different inner-being subjects: Phil 2:8 describes Christ's own inner humility and obedience-unto-death; Phil 3:10 describes the believer's inner identification with Christ's death. These are structurally different — one is divine inner-being, one is human participatory inner-being. The group description addresses Phil 3:10 only. Session B should assess whether Phil 2:8 belongs in this group or should be separately grouped under a divine-subject thanatos group.

Group 7423-003 (H4194 ma.vet): Hos 13:14 contains a textual and interpretive tension. The verse is cited in the positive register (ransom from Sheol, redemption from Death) but the final clause 'Compassion is hidden from my eyes' reads as divine determination to judge rather than redeem — a known crux. Paul cites Hos 13:14 in 1 Cor 15:55 in the positive/hope register; the OT context is more ambiguous. Session B should assess which register the verse is functioning in within the group's verse corpus, and whether the description's hopeful reading is warranted by the broader group evidence.
